docs.title: Community Maintained Fork
docs.text:
Welcome to the Motion Matching for Unity (MxM) documentation - a powerful animation system that brings cutting-edge motion matching technology to Unity.

Fork Status

This is a community-maintained fork with Unity 2022+ support. The original asset was made free and open-source by its creator, and this fork continues development and maintenance for modern Unity versions.

docs.title: What is Motion Matching?
docs.text:
Motion Matching is a revolutionary animation technique that creates fluid, responsive character movement by intelligently selecting the best animation frame from a large motion database in real-time.

Unlike traditional Finite State Machines (FSM) or blend trees, Motion Matching:

  • Eliminates manual transition setup - No more complex animator controllers
  • Provides natural responsiveness - Characters react instantly to player input
  • Scales with content - More animations = better results automatically
  • Handles complex locomotion - Smooth transitions between any movement states

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Unity Integration - Seamless workflow with Unity's animation system
  • Trajectory Prediction - Intelligent path planning for responsive movement
  • Tag System - Event-driven animation control
  • Performance Optimized - Efficient runtime with configurable quality settings
  • Modular Architecture - Easy integration with existing game systems

docs.title: Animation Authoring Guide
docs.text: A comprehensive guide for animators and technical artists preparing animation content for Motion Matching.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: Motion Matching fundamentally changes how animation content is authored and utilized. Unlike traditional state machines that require carefully timed loops and precise transitions, Motion Matching treats your animation library as a searchable database.

docs.title: Key Differences from Traditional Animation
docs.text:
Traditional FSM Motion Matching Perfect loops required Loops helpful but not required Exact transition timing critical System finds transitions automatically Limited reusability High reusability of poses State-based organization Data-based organization Manual transition crafting Automatic pose selection

The Animation Quality Principle

In Motion Matching, animation quality and variety matter more than organization and timing. Focus on natural, varied movement rather than perfect loops.

docs.title: Rigging Requirements
docs.text:
Humanoid Characters: - Unity Humanoid rig with proper T-pose - Consistent bone hierarchy across all clips - Proper hip/root bone setup for root motion

Generic Characters: - Consistent skeleton structure - Clearly defined root bone - Bone naming consistency across clips

docs.title: Frame Rate
docs.text:
Recommended: - 30 FPS or 60 FPS (consistent across all clips) - Avoid mixing frame rates in the same database

Acceptable: - Any frame rate works, but consistency is key - MxM samples at its own rate during pre-processing

docs.title: Clip Length
docs.text:
No strict requirements, but practical guidelines:

Clip Type Typical Length Notes Locomotion loops 1-3 seconds Shorter loops = more transition points Idle variations 2-5 seconds Can be non-looping Actions/Events 0.5-2 seconds Depends on action complexity Turns 0.3-1 second Quick directional changes

docs.title: Motion Capture (Recommended)
docs.text:
Advantages: - Natural weight and timing - Realistic foot contact and momentum - Subtle variations add richness - High pose diversity

Best practices: - Capture multiple takes of the same action - Encourage performer variation - Capture transitions between states - Include "connective tissue" movements

Common mocap cleanup: 1. Remove foot sliding 2. Stabilize ground contact 3. Clean up finger/hand jitter 4. Ensure root motion continuity

docs.title: Hand-Keyed Animation
docs.text:
Can work, but requires different approach:

Do: - Create natural timing with realistic acceleration - Add subtle secondary motion - Vary your animations (avoid copy-paste) - Think in terms of "movement phrases" not "states"

Don't: - Over-polish to perfection (variation is good) - Create identical mirrored clips (slight variation helps) - Force exact looping (MxM finds transitions) - Use overly stylized timing (unless intentional)

docs.title: Root Motion Configuration
docs.text: Root motion is critical for Motion Matching to work correctly.

docs.title: Unity Import Settings
docs.text: For each animation clip, configure:

docs.title: Animation Tab
docs.text:
Root Transform Rotation:
    ✓ Bake Into Pose
    Based Upon: Original
    Offset: 0

Root Transform Position (Y):
    ✓ Bake Into Pose
    Based Upon: Original
    Offset: 0

Root Transform Position (XZ):
    □ Bake Into Pose  (LEAVE UNCHECKED)
    Based Upon: Original

docs.title: Why These Settings?
docs.text:
Bake Rotation: - Character's facing direction comes from root motion - MxM controls rotation through trajectory

Bake Y Position: - Vertical motion (jumping, crouching) in animation - Prevents height drift

Don't Bake XZ: - Horizontal movement drives character forward - MxM reads this to calculate velocity - Critical for matching trajectory

docs.title: Verifying Root Motion
docs.text: Quick test: 1. Create empty scene 2. Add character with Animator 3. Play clip with root motion enabled 4. Character should move naturally across the floor 5. No sliding, skating, or floating

docs.title: Locomotion Set (Essential)
docs.text: A complete locomotion set for Motion Matching:

docs.title: Minimum Viable Set
docs.text:
Animation Variations Priority Idle 1-2 clips Essential Walk Forward 1 clip Essential Run Forward 1 clip Essential Walk Strafe Left/Right 2 clips High Run Strafe Left/Right 2 clips High 180° Turn 1-2 clips Medium Walk to Stop 1-2 clips Medium Idle to Walk 1-2 clips Medium

Total: 10-15 clips for basic locomotion

docs.title: Professional Quality Set
docs.text:
Expand with:

  • 8-directional movement (forward, back, strafe L/R, diagonal)
  • Speed variations (slow walk, jog, sprint)
  • Starts and stops (multiple variations)
  • Turns (45°, 90°, 135°, 180° both directions)
  • Transitional shuffles (weight shifting, readjustments)
  • Idle variations (breathing, looking around, fidgeting)

Total: 40-80 clips for rich locomotion

docs.title: Action/Event Animations
docs.text: For gameplay actions (attacks, interactions, etc.):

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
Every action animation should have clear phases:

┌─────────────┬───────────┬──────────────┬────────────┐
│   WIND-UP   │  ACTION   │ FOLLOW-THRU  │  RECOVERY  │
├─────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼────────────┤
│             │           │              │            │
│  Blend in   │ Mandatory │  Buffer      │ Can exit   │
│  from any   │ playback  │  zone        │ early      │
│  pose       │           │              │            │
└─────────────┴───────────┴──────────────┴────────────┘

Wind-Up (20-30% of clip): - Anticipation phase - System blends into this from locomotion - Doesn't need to start from specific pose

Action (30-40% of clip): - Core action (sword swing, button press, etc.) - Plays to completion without interruption - Most visually important section

Follow-Through (20-30% of clip): - Energy dissipation - Weight settles - Brief buffer before exiting

Recovery (10-20% of clip): - Return toward neutral/locomotion-ready pose - Can be interrupted early - Blends back to locomotion

docs.title: Event Animation Best Practices
docs.text:
Do: - Create natural wind-up that works from various poses - Include recovery phase to return to locomotion - Add variation (multiple attack animations, not just one) - Consider directional variants (left/right hand, forward/backward)

Don't: - Require exact starting pose - End abruptly without recovery - Create "moon walking" (feet sliding during action) - Ignore momentum and weight

docs.title: The Diversity Principle
docs.text:
10 varied clips > 50 similar clips

Motion Matching thrives on diversity in your animation set:

docs.title: Velocity Coverage
docs.text:
Ensure you have animations at different speeds:

  • 0 m/s: Idle, standing
  • 1-2 m/s: Slow walk, cautious movement
  • 3-4 m/s: Normal walk
  • 5-6 m/s: Jog, fast walk
  • 7-8 m/s: Run
  • 9+ m/s: Sprint

Why? MxM matches velocity as part of trajectory. Gaps in speed coverage = poor matching.

docs.title: Directional Coverage
docs.text:
Cover all movement directions:

      Forward (0°)
         ↑
         │
Left ←───┼───→ Right
  (270°) │  (90°)
         ↓
    Backward (180°)

Plus diagonals:
    45°, 135°, 225°, 315°

Minimum: Forward, back, left, right (4 directions) Better: + 4 diagonals (8 directions) Best: Full 16-direction coverage with speed variations

docs.title: Pose Variation
docs.text:
Even for similar actions, add variation:

Example: Idle Animations - Basic breathing idle - Look left/right idle - Shift weight idle - Fidget idle - Ready/alert idle

Why? Prevents repetitive-looking movement, adds life.

docs.title: Avoiding Gaps
docs.text:
Common coverage gaps:

  1. Transition zones
  2. Walk to run transitions
  3. Turn transitions
  4. Start/stop transitions

Solution: Add specific transition clips or ensure locomotion loops cover these naturally

  1. Speed gaps
  2. Having walk (3 m/s) and run (7 m/s) but nothing in between

Solution: Add jog or fast walk clips

  1. Directional gaps
  2. Forward/backward only, no strafing

Solution: Add strafe animations or accept limitation

docs.title: Foot Sliding Prevention
docs.text:
Cause: Root motion doesn't match foot contact

Solutions:

  1. Lock feet during contact
  2. Use foot IK constraints

  3. Ensure planted foot is truly stationary

  4. Match root velocity to feet

  5. Calculate speed from foot movement

  6. Adjust root motion to match

  7. In Unity:

  8. Use foot IK in MxM settings
  9. Increase foot joint weights in skeleton config

docs.title: Loop Continuity
docs.text:
While perfect loops aren't required, clean loops are better:

For looping clips:

  1. Match first/last frames:
  2. Root position
  3. Root rotation
  4. Limb positions

  5. Velocity

  6. Test the loop:

  7. Play 3+ cycles
  8. Watch for pops or discontinuities
  9. Check foot contact

Red flags: - Visible pop at loop point - Root position jump - Velocity change - Floating or ground penetration

docs.title: Motion Blur and Timing
docs.text:
Fast movements: - Use proper motion blur in mocap - Don't overcorrect natural blur - Preserve impact timing

Slow movements: - Ease in/out feels natural - Avoid linear timing - Add weight and momentum

docs.title: Tagging Strategy
docs.text: Tags are metadata you assign to clips for runtime filtering.

docs.title: Movement Speed Tags
docs.text:
• "Idle"
  • "Walk"
  • "Run"
  • "Sprint"

docs.title: Movement Style Tags
docs.text:
• "Combat" - combat-ready stance
  • "Casual" - relaxed movement
  • "Stealth" - crouched/sneaking
  • "Injured" - limping

docs.title: Directional Tags
docs.text:
• "Forward"
  • "Backward"
  • "Strafe"

docs.title: Contextual Tags
docs.text:
• "Indoor" - tight spaces
  • "Outdoor" - open areas
  • "Stairs" - vertical navigation
  • "Weapon" - armed animations

docs.title: Tag Usage Example
docs.text:
Clip: "Run_Forward_Combat.fbx"
Tags: ["Run", "Forward", "Combat", "Weapon"]

Runtime:
    - Player enters combat → Enable "Combat" tag
    - MxM now only uses combat-ready runs
    - Exit combat → Disable "Combat" tag
    - Smooth transition to casual runs

Best practices: - Use multiple tags per clip (3-5 typical) - Create tag hierarchy (broad to specific) - Plan tags before animation starts - Document tag meanings for team

docs.title: Quality Checklist
docs.text: Before exporting animations to Unity:

docs.title: Pre-Export Checklist
docs.text:
• Skeleton matches reference rig
  • Root bone properly defined
  • No unnecessary bones (keep hierarchy clean)
  • Foot IK solved (no sliding)
  • Loops are clean (if looping)
  • Root motion velocity is natural
  • Proper frame rate (30 or 60 FPS)
  • Named descriptively (RunForward_Combat_01.fbx)

docs.title: Post-Import Checklist (Unity)
docs.text:
• Rig type set (Humanoid/Generic)
  • Root transform settings correct
  • Animation compresses appropriately
  • Clip plays correctly in Animator
  • Root motion works (character moves naturally)
  • No warnings in console

docs.title: Post-Processing Checklist (MxM)
docs.text:
• Clip processes without errors (no red clips)
  • Pose count reasonable (check total poses)
  • Tags applied correctly
  • Blends look natural (test in play mode)
  • No popping or artifacts

docs.title: 1. "Perfect" Mirrored Clips
docs.text:
Problem: 

Walk_Left.fbx  ← Exact mirror of Walk_Right
Walk_Right.fbx

Issue: Identical mirrored clips reduce diversity.

Solution: Add subtle variation even in mirrors, or use asymmetric movement naturally.

docs.title: 2. T-Pose Contamination
docs.text:
Problem: Clips include T-pose frames at start/end.

Issue: MxM includes these in database, can match to T-pose!

Solution: Clean clips, remove setup frames before export.

docs.title: 3. Inconsistent Scale
docs.text:
Problem: Character scale differs between clips.

Issue: Root motion calculations are wrong.

Solution: Lock character scale at export (usually 1.0), verify in Unity.

docs.title: 4. Over-Polished Loops
docs.text:
Problem: Spent days perfecting a single loop.

Issue: Motion Matching benefits more from variety than perfection.

Solution: Time is better spent creating 3 "good enough" clips than 1 perfect clip.

docs.title: 5. Missing Connective Tissue
docs.text:
Problem: Only have clean loops: Idle, Walk, Run.

Issue: No natural transitions between states.

Solution: Capture transitions: Idle→Walk, Walk→Run, turning, stopping.

docs.title: 6. Unrealistic Foot Speed
docs.text:
Problem: Feet move too slow/fast for body velocity.

Issue: Creates sliding even with root motion.

Solution: Match foot speed to root speed exactly.

docs.title: Procedural Variation
docs.text:
Technique: Slightly modify duplicate clips procedurally.

Methods: - Time offset (start at different frames) - Speed variation (±5% playback speed) - Noise injection (subtle joint perturbation)

Use case: Create more variation from limited mocap.

docs.title: Stitching and Splicing
docs.text:
Technique: Cut and combine sections of different clips.

Example: 

Attack_WindUp_01 + Attack_Action_02 + Attack_Recovery_03
= New unique attack variant

Use case: Multiply action variations from existing library.

docs.title: Layered Animation
docs.text:
Technique: Combine locomotion with layered upper body.

Setup: - Full body locomotion in MxM - Upper body layer for actions (shooting, gesturing) - Use Unity's Animation Layers

Use case: Shoot while moving, gesture while walking.

docs.title: Performance Considerations for Animators
docs.text: Your animation choices affect runtime performance:

docs.title: Clip Length Impact
docs.text:
Longer clips: - More poses in database - Slower pre-processing - Larger memory footprint - Better coverage (can be worth it)

Recommendation: 2-5 seconds per clip is sweet spot.

docs.title: Pose Density
docs.text:
Controlled by Pose Interval setting (e.g., 0.1s):

  • Lower interval (0.05s) = More poses = Smoother but slower
  • Higher interval (0.2s) = Fewer poses = Faster but less smooth

Animator consideration: Dense, varied clips benefit from low intervals. Sparse clips can use higher intervals.

docs.title: Joint Count
docs.text:
More joints in skeleton config = slower matching.

Recommendation: - Start with 4-6 critical joints (hips, feet, hands) - Add more only if quality demands it - Prioritize joints that matter most (feet for locomotion)

docs.title: Iterative Approach
docs.text:
1. Phase 1: Proof of Concept
  2. 10-15 basic locomotion clips
  3. Quick pre-process test

  4. Verify movement works

  5. Phase 2: Expand Coverage

  6. Add directional variants
  7. Add speed variations

  8. Test for gaps

  9. Phase 3: Polish and Variation

  10. Add idle variations
  11. Add transition clips

  12. Refine tagging

  13. Phase 4: Actions and Events

  14. Add gameplay actions
  15. Configure event system
  16. Integrate with game systems

docs.title: Team Communication
docs.text:
For animation teams:

Document: - Naming conventions - Tag definitions - Required coverage (directions, speeds) - Clip specifications (length, FPS, format)

Share: - Coverage matrix (spreadsheet of what's needed) - Quality examples (reference clips) - Performance budgets (max clip count, sizes)

Iterate: - Regular playtest with real gameplay - Animator sees in-game, not just DCC tool - Quick feedback loop

docs.title: Recommended DCC Tools
docs.text:
Motion Capture: - MotionBuilder - Industry standard - Blender - Free alternative - Maya - Full animation pipeline

Hand-Keyed Animation: - Maya - Blender - 3ds Max

docs.title: Unity Tools
docs.text:
Built-in: - Animation window - Clip preview - Animator - Testing clips - Timeline - Sequencing

Third-Party: - Final IK - Foot IK solutions - Animation Baker - Root motion tools

docs.title: Analysis Tools
docs.text: In Unity: - Check root motion velocity (Animator window) - Preview in scene view - Use profiler to check pose counts

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Variety > Perfection - Diverse movement is more valuable than perfect loops
  2. Root motion is critical - Configure import settings correctly
  3. Coverage matters - Fill speed and directional gaps
  4. Natural movement wins - Realistic timing and weight transfer
  5. Tag strategically - Plan metadata for runtime flexibility
  6. Iterate quickly - Test early, test often

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Quick Start Guide - Implement animations in MxM
  • Technical Reference - Understand the system
  • FAQ - Troubleshoot common issues

You're Ready to Animate!

Armed with this guide, you can create animation content that takes full advantage of Motion Matching. Focus on natural, varied movement and let the system handle the transitions!

docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions
docs.text: Common questions and answers about Motion Matching for Unity.

docs.title: What is Motion Matching?
docs.text:
Motion Matching is a data-driven animation technique that selects the best animation frame from a large database in real-time based on the character's current state and desired trajectory. Unlike traditional state machines, it doesn't require manually setting up transitions between animations.

Key benefits: - No manual transition setup - Naturally responsive to player input - Scales automatically with more animation content - Handles complex locomotion smoothly

docs.title: How is MxM different from Unity's Mecanim?
docs.text: Feature Mecanim (FSM) MxM (Motion Matching) Setup Manual state graph Automated database Transitions Explicitly defined Automatically found Scalability Becomes complex with many states Improves with more data Responsiveness Limited by transition logic Instant frame-accurate matching Authoring Animation + logic work Primarily animation work

docs.title: Is MxM suitable for my project?
docs.text:
MxM excels for: - Third-person action games with complex movement - Games requiring realistic human locomotion - Projects with substantial animation budgets - Characters with varied terrain navigation

Consider alternatives for: - Simple 2D platformers - Projects with very limited animation sets (< 10 clips) - Stylized/exaggerated animation where timing is critical - Extremely performance-constrained platforms (low-end mobile)

docs.title: What's the performance cost?
docs.text:
MxM uses the Burst compiler and Jobs system for performance:

Typical performance (single character): - Desktop/Console: 0.5-2ms per frame - Mobile (high-end): 1-3ms per frame - Mobile (low-end): 3-6ms per frame

Optimization factors: - Pose database size (more poses = slower) - Skeleton joint count (more joints = slower) - Search frequency (every frame vs every 3 frames) - Number of active characters

docs.title: What Unity versions are supported?
docs.text:
• Minimum: Unity 2020.3 LTS
  • Recommended: Unity 2022.3 LTS or newer
  • Tested: Unity 6 (2023.2+)

This community fork maintains compatibility with modern Unity versions.

docs.title: Do I need the Burst compiler or Jobs system?
docs.text:
Yes, MxM relies on: - Burst Compiler for high-performance native code compilation - C# Jobs System for multi-threaded pose searching

Both are included by default in modern Unity versions.

docs.title: Can I use MxM with the new Input System?
docs.text:
Yes! MxM supports: - Unity's new Input System (recommended) - Legacy Input Manager - Custom input via scripting

Configure your preference in the Input Profile asset.

docs.title: How much animation data do I need?
docs.text:
Minimum viable: - 10-15 clips for basic locomotion - ~30 seconds of unique animation

Recommended: - 30-50 clips for quality locomotion - 2-5 minutes of motion capture data

Professional quality: - 100+ clips covering all scenarios - 10+ minutes of varied mocap

Quality vs Quantity

10 high-quality, varied clips beat 50 similar ones. Diversity in movement is key!

docs.title: What animation formats work best?
docs.text:
Best: - Motion capture (mocap) data - Keyframe animation with realistic timing - Animations with natural variation

Compatible but challenging: - Stylized/exaggerated animations - Hand-keyed animations with tight timing - Looping animations with exact cycle requirements

docs.title: Do animations need to be looping?
docs.text:
Not required! Unlike state machines, MxM doesn't rely on perfect loops.

However: - Looping clips provide more usable poses - Non-looping clips are fine (motion matching finds transitions anywhere) - Idle variations work great without loops

docs.title: How do I handle root motion?
docs.text:
Root motion should be baked into your animation clips.

Import settings for each clip: 1. Set Rig to Humanoid or Generic 2. Root Transform Rotation: Bake Into Pose 3. Root Transform Position (Y): Bake Into Pose 4. Root Transform Position (XZ): Based Upon → Original

MxM reads root motion from the clips during pre-processing.

docs.title: Can I use Generic rigs or only Humanoid?
docs.text:
Both work!

Humanoid: - Easier retargeting between characters - Better for realistic human characters - Required for some IK features

Generic: - More flexibility for non-human characters - Can use custom bone structures - May perform slightly better

docs.title: My animations are sliding or floating. Help!
docs.text:
Common causes:

  1. Root motion not configured correctly
  2. Check animation import settings

  3. Verify "Bake Into Pose" settings

  4. Trajectory speed mismatch

  5. Trajectory Generator Max Speed doesn't match animation speeds

  6. Adjust Max Speed in Input Profile

  7. Ground contact issues

  8. Foot joints not weighted properly in skeleton setup

  9. Increase foot joint weights to 1.0

  10. Character controller interference

  11. Character Controller or Rigidbody fighting with MxM
  12. Disable physics-based movement when using MxM

docs.title: How long does pre-processing take?
docs.text:
Typical times: - 10 clips, 0.1s interval: ~5 minutes - 50 clips, 0.1s interval: ~20 minutes - 100 clips, 0.05s interval: ~45+ minutes

Factors: - Number of clips - Total clip duration - Pose interval (lower = more poses = longer) - Number of joints in skeleton - CPU performance

docs.title: Do I need to re-process after every change?
docs.text:
Yes, re-process when: - Animation clips are modified - Clips are added or removed - Skeleton configuration changes - Pose interval or trajectory settings change

No re-processing needed when: - Changing runtime settings (blend time, etc.) - Modifying Input Profiles - Adjusting tags at runtime - Changing character controller settings

docs.title: Can I process in the background?
docs.text:
Unfortunately no. Unity editor will be unresponsive during processing.

Best practices: - Process during lunch breaks or end of day - Start with subset of clips for testing - Only do full processing when needed - Consider multiple Anim Data assets for iteration

docs.title: What does "Red Clip" mean after processing?
docs.text:
Red clips indicate errors:

Common causes: 1. Looping discontinuity - Start/end frames don't match 2. Missing root motion - No movement data found 3. Skeleton mismatch - Joints specified don't exist 4. Corrupted animation data - Re-import the clip

Check the Console for specific error messages.

docs.title: Character isn't moving when I press input
docs.text: Checklist: - [ ] Anim Data is assigned to MxMAnimator - [ ] Anim Data is fully pre-processed (no errors) - [ ] Input Profile is assigned to Trajectory Generator - [ ] Input Profile has correct input bindings - [ ] Animations have root motion - [ ] Character has MxMAnimator and Trajectory Generator components

docs.title: Movement feels sluggish or delayed
docs.text: Solutions: 1. Increase Responsiveness in Trajectory Generator (try 0.9) 2. Decrease Blend Time in MxMAnimator (try 0.2s) 3. Ensure Search Frequency is "Every Frame" 4. Check animation speeds match desired movement speed

docs.title: Animations look jerky or stuttery
docs.text: Solutions: 1. Increase Blend Time (try 0.4-0.5s) 2. Decrease Pose Interval (more poses = smoother) 3. Add more animation variations 4. Ensure consistent clip FPS (all 30fps or all 60fps)

docs.title: Can I use MxM with multiple characters?
docs.text:
Yes! Each character needs: - Its own MxMAnimator component - Its own Trajectory Generator - Can share the same Anim Data asset

Performance note: Each character adds processing cost. Test on target hardware with max expected character count.

docs.title: How do I optimize for mobile?
docs.text:
Optimization strategies:

  1. Increase Pose Interval to 0.15-0.2s
  2. Reduce Search Frequency to every 2-3 frames
  3. Fewer trajectory points (2-3 instead of 4-5)
  4. Fewer skeleton joints (4-6 instead of 10+)
  5. Smaller animation database (30-40 clips max)
  6. LOD system - Disable MxM for distant characters

docs.title: How do Tags work?
docs.text:
Tags are labels you assign to animation clips that can be enabled/disabled at runtime.

Example: 

// Enable "Combat" tagged animations
mxmAnimator.EnableTag("Combat");

// Disable "Run" tagged animations (force walk)
mxmAnimator.DisableTag("Run");

// Only allow "Crouch" animations
mxmAnimator.SetRequiredTag("Crouch");

Use cases: - Switch between combat/exploration movement - Force walking in interiors - Contextual animations based on game state

docs.title: Can I blend between different Anim Data assets?
docs.text:
Not directly. Each MxMAnimator uses one Anim Data at a time.

Workarounds: - Create one large Anim Data with all clips - Use Tags to filter which clips are active - Swap Anim Data assets at runtime (causes brief pause)

docs.title: How do I trigger specific animations?
docs.text:
MxM finds animations automatically, but you can guide it:

Option 1: Events System - Create Event composite with specific animations - Trigger event via code: mxmAnimator.TriggerEvent("Attack");

Option 2: Required Tags - Tag specific clips - Require that tag: mxmAnimator.SetRequiredTag("SpecialMove");

Option 3: Trajectory Manipulation - Shape the desired trajectory to match specific clips - Works best for locomotion variants

docs.title: Can I use MxM with IK (Inverse Kinematics)?
docs.text:
Yes! MxM works with Unity's IK system.

Setup: 1. Enable IK on your Animator 2. Implement OnAnimatorIK() in your script 3. Apply IK after MxM updates the pose

Common use cases: - Foot placement on uneven terrain - Hand placement on objects - Head look-at targets

docs.title: Does MxM support animation events?
docs.text:
Yes, through the Event System.

Setup: 1. Define events in your Anim Data 2. Configure event conditions (tags, windows) 3. Subscribe to event callbacks in code

Use for: - Footstep sounds - Particle effects - Gameplay triggers - Combat hit detection

docs.title: "No valid pose found" error
docs.text:
Causes: - No animations match current requirements - All matching clips filtered by tags - Trajectory too extreme for available animations

Solutions: - Add more animation variety - Check tag requirements aren't too restrictive - Reduce trajectory extremes - Verify clips are processed correctly

docs.title: Character "pops" or teleports
docs.text:
Causes: - Frame discontinuity in animation clips - Blend time too short - Missing transition coverage

Solutions: - Increase Blend Time - Add more clips covering transitions - Check for corrupted animation data - Verify pose interval isn't too high

docs.title: Feet not planted properly
docs.text: Solutions: 1. Increase foot joint weights to 1.0 2. Use IK foot placement for terrain adaptation 3. Add more grounded animations 4. Check root motion settings

docs.title: Compile errors after importing
docs.text:
For Unity 2022+: - This fork is updated for modern Unity - Pull latest version from repository - Check for conflicting packages

For older Unity: - Consider using older MxM version - Or help update compatibility (PRs welcome!)

docs.title: What's the recommended workflow for adding new animations?
docs.text:
1. Import new clips with correct settings
  2. Add to existing composite in Anim Data
  3. Tag appropriately for organization
  4. Re-process the Anim Data
  5. Test in Play mode
  6. Iterate - adjust tags, weights, settings

docs.title: Should I create multiple Anim Data assets or one large one?
docs.text:
Separate Anim Data assets when: - Different characters with unique skeletons - Completely different animation sets (human vs creature) - Want to swap entire animation sets at runtime

Single Anim Data when: - Same character with many animations - Want seamless blending across all animations - Tag system can handle filtering

docs.title: How do I test changes without full re-processing?
docs.text: Iteration strategies: 1. Create test Anim Data with subset of clips 2. Test with smaller clip set first 3. Use low Pose Interval initially (0.2s) for fast processing 4. Do final processing with full quality settings once happy

docs.title: Can I version control the processed Anim Data?
docs.text:
Anim Data assets are binary and large (100MB+)

Recommendations: - Git LFS for tracking binary assets - Or exclude from version control and re-process per machine - Document exact processing settings for reproducibility

docs.title: Can I migrate from Mecanim to MxM?
docs.text:
Yes! Many projects have migrated successfully.

Migration steps: 1. Keep existing Animator component 2. Add MxMAnimator alongside 3. MxM will override Animator playback 4. Original Animator Controller becomes unused

Benefits: - Can switch back easily - Preserve existing animation clip organization - Gradual migration path

docs.title: Can I use both MxM and traditional animation?
docs.text:
Yes! You can mix: - MxM for locomotion (complex movement) - Animator for cutscenes (precise timing) - Timeline for cinematics (exact choreography)

Toggle MxMAnimator component on/off to switch control.

docs.title: Will this work with Unity 6?
docs.text:
Yes, this community fork is tested with Unity 6 (2023.2+).

If you encounter issues, please report them on GitHub.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This guide will walk you through installing Motion Matching for Unity (MxM) and setting up your first motion matching character.

docs.title: Unity Version
docs.text:
• Minimum: Unity 2020.3 LTS
  • Recommended: Unity 2022.3 LTS or newer
  • Tested: Unity 2022.3+, Unity 6

Unity 2022+ Compatibility

This community fork has been updated for Unity 2022+ compatibility. If you encounter issues with newer Unity versions, please report them on GitHub.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
MxM integrates with Unity's built-in systems:

  • Unity Animation System (Mecanim)
  • Unity Physics (for trajectory prediction)
  • .NET Standard 2.1 or newer

docs.title: Project Requirements
docs.text:
• Animation clips - Humanoid or generic animated characters
  • Recommended: Motion capture data for best results
  • Storage: Motion databases can be large (100MB+ for full character sets)

docs.title: Option 1: Install via Git URL (Recommended)
docs.text:
1. Open your Unity project
  2. Go to Window > Package Manager
  3. Click the + button in the top-left corner
  4. Select Add package from git URL
  5. Enter the following URL:
https://github.com/Frost-Blade-Studios/Motion-Matching-for-Unity.git
  1. Click Add
  2. Unity will download and install MxM

Keep Updated

To update to the latest version, simply remove and re-add the package using the same Git URL.

docs.title: Option 2: Manual Installation
docs.text:
1. Download or clone this repository:
git clone https://github.com/Frost-Blade-Studios/Motion-Matching-for-Unity.git

  1. Copy the MxM folder to your Unity project's Assets directory

  2. Unity will automatically import the package

docs.title: Option 3: Unity Package Manager (Local)
docs.text:
1. Clone this repository to your local machine
  2. In Unity, open Window > Package Manager
  3. Click + > Add package from disk
  4. Navigate to the cloned repository
  5. Select the package.json file (if available)

docs.title: Verifying Installation
docs.text:
After installation, verify MxM is working:

  1. Check the MxM menu: You should see MxM in the Unity menu bar
  2. Check for components: Search for "MxM" in the Add Component menu
  3. Open example scenes: If included, navigate to MxM/Examples to see demo scenes

docs.title: Step 1: Prepare Your Character
docs.text:
MxM works with humanoid or generic animated characters. You'll need:

  1. Character model with a rig
  2. Animation clips (minimum 10-20 clips recommended)
  3. Animator Controller (can be basic or empty)

Animation Requirements

For a basic locomotion setup, you'll need:

  • Idle animations (1-2 clips)
  • Walk cycle (multiple directions if possible)
  • Run cycle (multiple directions if possible)
  • Turn animations (optional but recommended)
  • Start/stop animations (optional but recommended)

docs.title: Step 2: Create an MxM Animator
docs.text:
1. Select your character in the Hierarchy
  2. Add the MxMAnimator component:
  3. Click Add Component
  4. Search for "MxMAnimator"

  5. Click to add

  6. Configure the component:

  7. Assign your Animator reference
  8. Set the Update Mode (typically "Normal")
  9. Configure Trajectory settings

docs.title: Step 3: Create an Anim Data Asset
docs.text:
The Anim Data asset is the heart of MxM - it stores your processed animation database.

  1. Create the asset:
  2. Right-click in Project window
  3. Create > MxM > Anim Data

  4. Name it (e.g., "CharacterAnimData")

  5. Add animation clips:

  6. Select your Anim Data asset
  7. In the Inspector, click "Add Clips"

  8. Select all your animation clips

  9. Configure poses:

  10. Set Pose Interval (default: 0.1s works well)
  11. Configure Trajectory Points for prediction

  12. Set Joint Weights (hands, feet usually higher priority)

  13. Process the data:

  14. Click "Pre-Process"
  15. Wait for the process to complete (can take several minutes)
  16. A progress bar will show status

Processing Time

Pre-processing can take 5-30 minutes depending on:

  • Number of animation clips
  • Length of animations
  • Pose interval settings
  • Your computer's performance

docs.title: Step 4: Assign Anim Data to MxMAnimator
docs.text:
1. Select your character in the Hierarchy
  2. Find the MxMAnimator component
  3. Drag your processed Anim Data asset to the Anim Data field

docs.title: Step 5: Set Up Input & Trajectory
docs.text:
MxM needs to know where your character wants to go:

  1. Create an Input Profile:
  2. Right-click in Project window
  3. Create > MxM > Input Profile

  4. Configure input bindings (WASD, gamepad, etc.)

  5. Assign to MxMAnimator:

  6. Drag the Input Profile to the MxMAnimator

  7. Configure Trajectory Generator settings

  8. Test movement:

  9. Enter Play mode
  10. Use input to move your character
  11. MxM should select appropriate animations automatically

docs.title: Unity 2022+ Migration Notes
docs.text: If you're upgrading a project from an older Unity version:

docs.title: Breaking Changes
docs.text:
• API Updates: Some deprecated Unity APIs have been updated
  • Serialization: Re-process your Anim Data assets after upgrading
  • Physics: Trajectory prediction may need recalibration

docs.title: Recommended Steps
docs.text:
1. Backup your project before upgrading Unity
  2. Upgrade Unity to 2022.3 LTS or newer
  3. Re-import MxM from this fork
  4. Re-process all Anim Data assets
  5. Test thoroughly in Play mode
  6. Recalibrate trajectory and timing settings if needed

docs.title: "MxMAnimator not found"
docs.text:
• Ensure the package is installed correctly
  • Check for compilation errors in the Console
  • Try reimporting the package

docs.title: "No valid pose found"
docs.text:
• Check that Anim Data is assigned to MxMAnimator
  • Verify Anim Data has been pre-processed
  • Ensure animation clips are compatible with your character rig

docs.title: Performance Issues
docs.text:
• Reduce Pose Interval (larger intervals = fewer poses = faster)
  • Decrease Trajectory Points
  • Lower Search Budget in MxMAnimator settings
  • Use LOD system for distant characters

docs.title: Animations Look Jerky
docs.text:
• Decrease Pose Interval (more poses = smoother)
  • Increase Blend Time in MxMAnimator
  • Check that Update Mode is set appropriately
  • Verify Frame Rate of source animations

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Now that MxM is installed and set up:

  1. Explore the examples (if included in the package)
  2. Read the Technical Reference for deep understanding
  3. Check the Original PDFs for detailed workflows
  4. Experiment with different settings and animation sets
  5. Join the community and share your results!

docs.title: Additional Resources
docs.text:
• GitHub Repository: Frost-Blade-Studios/Motion-Matching-for-Unity
  • Report Issues: GitHub Issues
  • Community Discussions: GitHub Discussions

Ready to Go!

Your MxM setup is complete! Try creating a simple character with basic locomotion animations to see motion matching in action.

docs.title: Quick Start Guide
docs.text: Get your first Motion Matching character up and running in 30 minutes.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting, ensure you have:

  • Unity 2020.3 LTS or newer (2022.3 LTS recommended)
  • MxM installed - See Installation Guide
  • Humanoid or Generic rigged character with animation clips
  • 10-20 animation clips minimum (locomotion recommended for first test)

First Time with Motion Matching?

Motion Matching works differently than traditional animators. Instead of setting up state machines and transitions, you'll be creating an animation database that the system searches in real-time.

docs.title: What You Need
docs.text:
For a basic locomotion setup, gather these animation types:

Animation Type Quantity Examples Idle 1-2 clips Standing idle, breathing Walk 2-4 clips Walk forward, walk strafe left/right Run 2-4 clips Run forward, run strafe left/right Turns 2-4 clips (optional) Turn 90°, turn 180° Starts/Stops 2-4 clips (optional) Start from idle, stop to idle

Animation Requirements

  • All clips must use the same rig/skeleton
  • Clips should have consistent FPS (30 or 60 recommended)
  • Root motion should be baked into the animations
  • Motion capture data produces the best results

docs.title: Import Settings
docs.text:
For each animation clip:

  1. Select the animation file in Unity
  2. In the Inspector, set:
  3. Rig: Humanoid (or Generic if appropriate)
  4. Root Transform Rotation: Bake Into Pose
  5. Root Transform Position (Y): Bake Into Pose
  6. Root Transform Position (XZ): Based Upon → Original
  7. Click Apply

docs.title: Step 2: Create the MxM Anim Data Asset
docs.text: The Anim Data asset is the heart of MxM - it's your pre-processed animation database.

docs.title: Create the Asset
docs.text:
1. Right-click in your Project window
  2. Create → MxM → Anim Data
  3. Name it descriptively (e.g., PlayerLocomotion_AnimData)

docs.title: Configure Basic Settings
docs.text: Select your new Anim Data asset and configure:

docs.title: General Settings
docs.text: Setting Value Notes Pose Interval 0.1 seconds Lower = more poses = smoother but slower Blending Mode Inertial Blending Standard for most characters

docs.title: Skeleton Configuration
docs.text:
1. Click "Configure Skeleton"
  2. Select key joints for matching:

Minimum Setup: - Hips (root) - Left Foot - Right Foot

Recommended Setup (better quality): - Hips (root) - Left Foot / Right Foot - Left Hand / Right Hand - Head (optional)

  1. Set Joint Weights:
  2. Feet: 1.0 (critical for foot placement)
  3. Hands: 0.7 (important for upper body)
  4. Hips: 0.8 (critical for root motion)
  5. Head: 0.5 (less critical)

docs.title: Trajectory Configuration
docs.text:
Configure how far the system looks ahead:

Trajectory Point Time Offset Recommended Weight Point 1 0.2s ahead 1.0 Point 2 0.4s ahead 0.8 Point 3 0.6s ahead 0.6

Trajectory Points

More trajectory points = better prediction but slower search. 3 points is a good starting balance.

docs.title: Step 3: Add Animation Clips
docs.text: Now add your animation clips to the database:

docs.title: Create a Composite
docs.text:
Composites group related animations together.

  1. In your Anim Data asset, click "Add Composite"
  2. Name it (e.g., "Locomotion")
  3. Set Type: Standard Composite

docs.title: Add Clips to Composite
docs.text:
1. Click "Add Clips" in the composite section
  2. Select all your locomotion animation clips
  3. Click "Add"

docs.title: Configure Clips
docs.text:
For each clip in the list:

  1. Review settings - Most defaults are fine
  2. Tag clips (optional but recommended):
  3. Add tag "Walk" to walk clips
  4. Add tag "Run" to run clips
  5. Add tag "Idle" to idle clips

Tags are powerful

Tags let you control which animations are available at runtime. For example, you can disable "Run" animations when the character is crouching.

docs.title: Step 4: Pre-Process the Database
docs.text: This is where MxM analyzes your animations and builds the searchable database.

docs.title: Start Processing
docs.text:
1. Scroll to the bottom of your Anim Data inspector
  2. Click "Pre-Process"
  3. Wait - This can take 5-30 minutes depending on:
  4. Number of clips
  5. Clip lengths
  6. Pose interval setting
  7. Your computer's performance

docs.title: What's Happening
docs.text:
During pre-processing, MxM:

  1. Samples poses at the specified interval (every 0.1s)
  2. Calculates trajectories for each pose
  3. Builds feature vectors for fast searching
  4. Creates acceleration structures (KD-trees)

Processing Time

  • 10 clips @ 0.1s interval: ~5 minutes
  • 50 clips @ 0.1s interval: ~15-20 minutes
  • 100 clips @ 0.05s interval: ~30+ minutes

Go grab coffee - this is a one-time setup!

docs.title: Verify Success
docs.text:
After processing:

  • Green clips: Successfully processed
  • Red clips: Errors (check console for details)
  • Pose count: Should show total poses extracted

docs.title: Step 5: Set Up Your Character
docs.text: Now let's apply MxM to your character in the scene.

docs.title: Add the Character
docs.text:
1. Drag your character into the scene
  2. Ensure it has an Animator component
  3. The Animator Controller can be empty or basic

docs.title: Add MxMAnimator Component
docs.text:
1. Select your character in the Hierarchy
  2. Click Add Component
  3. Search for "MxMAnimator"
  4. Click to add

docs.title: Configure MxMAnimator
docs.text: In the MxMAnimator component:

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Anim Data: Drag your PlayerLocomotion_AnimData asset here
  2. Animator: Auto-assigned (or drag your Animator)
  3. Transform: Auto-assigned (root transform)

docs.title: Runtime Settings
docs.text: Setting Recommended Value Description Update Mode Normal Standard update timing Blend Time 0.3 seconds Transition smoothness Playback Speed 1.0 Normal speed Search Frequency Every Frame Best quality (can optimize later)

docs.title: Step 6: Set Up Input and Trajectory
docs.text: MxM needs to know where the character wants to go.

docs.title: Add Trajectory Generator
docs.text:
1. Select your character
  2. Click Add Component
  3. Search for "MxMTrajectoryGenerator"
  4. Click to add

docs.title: Create Input Profile
docs.text:
1. Right-click in Project window
  2. Create → MxM → Input Profile
  3. Name it (e.g., PlayerInputProfile)

docs.title: Configure Input Profile
docs.text:
Select your Input Profile:

  1. Input Type: Choose based on your project:
  2. Unity Input System (new Input System)
  3. Legacy Input Manager (old Input.GetAxis)

  4. Custom (scripted input)

  5. Movement Bindings:

  6. Horizontal Axis: "Horizontal" (A/D or Left/Right)

  7. Vertical Axis: "Vertical" (W/S or Up/Down)

  8. Trajectory Settings:

Setting Value Description Max Speed 5.0 Maximum movement speed Acceleration 10.0 How quickly speed ramps up Responsiveness 0.8 Path prediction smoothness

docs.title: Link Input to Trajectory Generator
docs.text:
1. Select your character
  2. In MxMTrajectoryGenerator:
  3. Input Profile: Drag your PlayerInputProfile
  4. MxM Animator: Drag the MxMAnimator (or auto-link)

docs.title: Step 7: Test Your Character
docs.text: Time to see motion matching in action!

docs.title: Enter Play Mode
docs.text:
1. Click Play in Unity
  2. Use WASD or arrow keys to move

docs.title: What to Expect
docs.text:
If working correctly: - Character smoothly blends between animations - Movement feels responsive to input - Transitions happen automatically without setup - Foot placement looks natural

If something's wrong: - Character frozen: Check Anim Data is assigned - Jerky movement: Increase Blend Time - No response to input: Verify Input Profile settings - Sliding feet: Re-check root motion settings on clips

docs.title: Character Doesn't Move
docs.text: Check: - Is Anim Data assigned to MxMAnimator? - Is Anim Data fully pre-processed (no red clips)? - Is Input Profile assigned to Trajectory Generator? - Do animation clips have root motion enabled?

docs.title: Animations Look Jerky
docs.text: Solutions: - Increase Blend Time (try 0.4-0.5s) - Decrease Pose Interval (more poses = smoother) - Check animation FPS consistency

docs.title: Character Slides/Doesn't Follow Trajectory
docs.text: Check: - Root motion settings on animation clips - Trajectory Generator Max Speed matches animation speeds - Character Controller or Rigidbody isn't conflicting

docs.title: Poor Performance / Low FPS
docs.text: Optimize: - Increase Pose Interval (fewer poses = faster) - Reduce Trajectory Points - Lower Search Frequency (every 2-3 frames) - Reduce number of joints in skeleton setup

docs.title: Expand Your Setup
docs.text:
Now that you have basic locomotion working:

  1. Add more animations - Jumps, attacks, interactions
  2. Use Tags - Create contextual animation sets
  3. Create Events - Trigger gameplay events from animations
  4. Tune quality - Adjust weights and timings

docs.title: Learn More
docs.text:
• Technical Reference - Deep dive into how MxM works
  • Animation Authoring Guide - Optimize your animation content
  • User Manual - Complete feature reference

docs.title: Quick Reference Checklist
docs.text:
Use this checklist for future characters:

  • Import animation clips with correct settings
  • Create Anim Data asset
  • Configure skeleton (joints + weights)
  • Configure trajectory points
  • Add clips to composite
  • Pre-process database (wait for completion)
  • Add MxMAnimator to character
  • Assign Anim Data reference
  • Add MxMTrajectoryGenerator
  • Create and assign Input Profile
  • Test in Play mode
  • Tune blend time and responsiveness

docs.title: For Desktop/Console Games
docs.text:
• Pose Interval: 0.05 - 0.1 seconds
  • Search Frequency: Every frame
  • Trajectory Points: 4-5 points
  • Joints: 8-12 joints (full body)

docs.title: For Mobile Games
docs.text:
• Pose Interval: 0.15 - 0.2 seconds
  • Search Frequency: Every 2-3 frames
  • Trajectory Points: 2-3 points
  • Joints: 4-6 joints (lower body focus)

docs.title: For VR Games
docs.text:
• Pose Interval: 0.08 - 0.12 seconds
  • Search Frequency: Every frame (90fps target)
  • Trajectory Points: 3-4 points
  • Blend Time: 0.2s (snappier for responsiveness)

You're Ready!

You now have a working motion matching character! Experiment with different settings, add more animations, and explore the advanced features in the Technical Reference.
